concepts.title: Principle of Superposition
concepts.conceptBody:
Principle of Superposition

Two or more waves can travel simultaneously in a medium without affecting the motion of one another. Therefore, the resultant displacement of each particle of the medium at any instant is equal to the vector sum of the displacements produced by the two waves separately. This principle is called 'principle of superposition'. It holds for all types of waves, provided the waves are not of very large amplitude. We can express the superposition principle in the form

$\text{y}\left( \text{x},\text{t} \right)={{\text{y}}_{1}}\left( \text{x},\text{t} \right)+{{\text{y}}_{2}}\left( \text{x},\text{t} \right)+\ldots +{{\text{y}}_{\text{n}}}\left( \text{x},\text{t} \right)$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y\left( x,t \right)=\sum _{J=1}^{n}{{y}_{j}}\left( x,t \right)$

Here, the ${{\text{y}}_{\text{j}}}$ are the individual wave functions, and their sum, the wave function $\text{y}\left( \text{x},\text{t} \right)$ describes the resultant behavior of the medium as a function of position and time.

concepts.title: Interference of Waves
concepts.conceptBody:
Interference

Consider the superposition of two sinusoidal waves of same frequency at a point. Let us assume that the two waves are travelling in the same direction with same velocity. The equation of the two waves reaching at a point can be written as,

${{\text{y}}_{1}}={{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{sin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)$ and

${{\text{y}}_{2}}={{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$

The resultant displacement of the point where the waves meet is

$y ={{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{y}}_{2}}$

$={{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{sin}\left( \alpha -\omega \text{t} \right)+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$

$={{\text{A}}_{1}}\text{sin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)\text{cos}\phi +{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)\text{sin}\phi$

$ =\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi \right)\text{sin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\phi \text{cos}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)$

$=\text{Acos}\theta \text{sin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)+\text{Asin}\theta \text{cos}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t} \right)$

Or $y =\text{Asin}\left( \text{kx}-\omega \text{t}+\phi \right)$

Here, ${{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi =\text{Acos}\theta$

and ${{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\phi =\text{Asin}\theta$

or ${{\text{A}}^{2}}$ $={{\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\phi \right)}^{2}}$

or $\text{A}$ $=\sqrt{\text{A}_{1}^{2}+\text{A}_{2}^{2}+2 {{\text{A}}_{1}} {{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi }$ … (1)

and $ \text{ tan}\theta =\frac{\text{Asin}\theta }{\text{Acos}\theta }=\frac{{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{sin}\phi }{{{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}\text{cos}\phi }$

 

The above result can be obtained by graphical method as well. Assume a vector ${{\vec{A}}_{1}}$ of length ${{A}_{1}}$ to represent the amplitude of first wave.

Another vector ${{\vec{A}}_{2}}$ of length ${{\text{A}}_{2}}$, making an angle $\phi $ with ${{\vec{A}}_{1}}$ represents the amplitude of second wave. The resultant of ${{\vec{A}}_{1}}$ and ${{\vec{A}}_{2}}$ represent the amplitude of resulting function $\text{y}$. The angle $\theta $ represents the phase difference between the resulting function and the first wave.

Now we know that intensity of a wave is given by :

$\text{I}=\frac{1}{2}\rho {{\text{A}}^{2}}{{\omega }^{2}}\text{v}$

i.e. $I\propto {{\text{A}}^{2}}$

So, if $\rho ,\omega $ and $v$ are same for both interfering waves, Eq. (i) can also be written as,

$\text{I}={{\text{I}}_{1}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}}\text{cos}\phi$ … (ii)

we see that the resulting amplitude A and intensity I depends on the phase difference $\phi $ between the interfering wave. Where $\text{cos}\phi =+1, \text{ A}={{\text{A}}_{\text{max}}}={{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}}$

or $\text{I}={{\text{I}}_{\text{max}}}={{\left( \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}+\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}}$

and the waves are said to be interfering constructively.

Similarly where $\text{cos}\phi =-1,  \text{ A}={{\text{A}}_{\text{min } }}={{\text{A}}_{1}}\sim {{\text{A}}_{2}}$

Or $\text{I}={{\text{I}}_{\text{min}}}={{\left( \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}-\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}}$

and the waves are said to be interfering destructively.

Note: 

(1) All maxima are equally spaced (as path difference between two consecutive maxima is $\lambda $ ) and equally loud $\left[ {{I}_{\text{max}}}={{\left( \sqrt{{{I}_{1}}}+\sqrt{{{I}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}} \right]$. Same is also true for minima with ${{\text{I}}_{\text{min}}}={{\left( \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}\sim \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}}$. Also interference maxima and minima are alternate as for maxima $\Delta\text{ x}=0,\lambda ,2\lambda$ etc., while for minima $\Delta\text{ x}=\left( \lambda /2 \right),\left( 3\lambda /2 \right)$, etc. This all is shown graphically.

(2) $\frac{{{I}_{\text{max}}}}{{{I}_{\text{min}}}}=\frac{{{\left( \sqrt{{{I}_{1}}}+\sqrt{{{I}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}}}{{{\left( \sqrt{{{I}_{1}}}\sim \sqrt{{{I}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}}}\frac{{{\left( {{\text{A}}_{1}}+{{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}{{{\left(  {{\text{A}}_{1}}\sim {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}$ with $\frac{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}{{{\text{I}}_{2}}}=\frac{\text{A}_{1}^{2}}{ \text{ A}_{2}^{2}}$

So if ${{I}_{1}}$ and ${{I}_{2}}$ or ${{A}_{1}}$ and ${{A}_{2}}$ are given $\left( {{I}_{\text{max}}}/{{I}_{\text{min}}} \right)$ can be calculated and vice-versa. From the above it is also clear than if ${{\text{I}}_{1}}={{\text{I}}_{2}}={{\text{I}}_{0}}$.

${{\text{I}}_{\text{max}}}={{\left( \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{0}}}+\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{0}}} \right)}^{2}}=4{{\text{I}}_{0}}$ and ${{\text{I}}_{\text{min } }}={{\left( \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{0}}}-\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{0}}} \right)}^{2}}=\text{O}$

i.e., in maxima intensity will be 4-times that of a single wave $\left( {{\text{I}}_{0}} \right)$ while intensity of minima is zero if the interfering waves are of equal intensities.

(3) In interference the intensity of maxima ${{\left( \sqrt{{{I}_{1}}}+\sqrt{{{I}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}}$ exceeds the sum of individual intensities

$\left( {{\text{I}}_{1}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}} \right)$ by an amount $2\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}}$ while of minima ${{\left( \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}\sim \sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{2}}} \right)}^{2}}$ lacks $\left( {{\text{I}}_{1}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}} \right)$ by the same

amount $2\sqrt{{{\text{I}}_{1}}{{\text{I}}_{2}}}$

Hence, we conclude that in interference energy is neither created nor destroyed but is redistributed.

(4) Here we had assumed that the two waves from ${{S}_{1}}$ and ${{S}_{2}}$ start in the same phase. Hence, at $\text{P}$ they have a constant phase difference $\phi =\left( 2\pi /\lambda \right)\Delta\text{ x}$, developed due to different paths traversed by them. Such waves are said to be 'Coherent' and produce sustained interference effects. However, if there is an initial phase difference between the waves ${{\phi }_{0}}$ then $\phi =$ ${{\phi }_{0}}+\left( 2\pi /\lambda \right)\Delta\text{ x}$ and if ${{\phi }_{0}}$ is not constant and varies rapidly and randomly with time, at $\text{P}$ sometimes constructive and sometimes destructive interference will take place so that ${{\text{I}}_{\text{av}}}=\frac{1}{2}\left( {{\text{I}}_{\text{max}}}+{{\text{I}}_{\text{min}}} \right)=\left( {{\text{I}}_{1}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}} \right)$ and hence, no interference effect is observed. Such waves are called 'Incoherent'.

So for observing interference effects waves must be coherent.

concepts.title: Reflection and Refraction of Wave
concepts.conceptBody:
Reflection and Refraction of Wave

The nature of the reflected & transmitted wave depends on the nature of end point. There are three possibilities.

(a) End point is fixed: Waves on reflection from a fixed end undergoes a phase change of 180.

(b) End point is free : There is no phase change in waves on reflection. 
 

(c) End point is neither completely fixed nor completely free 

For example, consider a light string attached to a heavier string as shown in figure if a wave pulse is produced on the light string moving towards the junction, a part of the reflected and a part is transmitted on the heavier string. The reflected wave is inverted with to the original one (figure (a).

On the other hand, if the wave is produced on the heavier string, which moves towards the junction, a part will be reflected and a transmitted, no inversion of wave shape will take place (as shown in figure (b).

So the rule is: if a wave enters a region where the wave velocity is smaller, the reflected wave is inverted. If it enters a region where the wave velocity is larger, the reflected wave is not inverted. The transmitted wave is never inverted.
